text: The Da Vinci robotic system is used for a single-site cholecystectomy. The surgeon, seated at the console, begins dissecting Calot's triangle. A bedside assistant uses a grasper to retract the gallbladder fundus and liver superiorly. The Maryland Grasper and Dissector in arm two on the right of the screen performs the dissection, while the Cobra Grasper in arm one on the left grasps the base of the gallbladder or Hartman's pouch.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection of the cystic duct using a Maryland dissector, with stable visualization provided by the laparoscopic camera. The single site instruments demonstrate mobility and flexibility, crossing when necessary to facilitate dissection maneuvers.

The single-site robotic instruments are visible, demonstrating mobility and proficiency in grasping and retracting tissues. Despite lacking articulation or wrist action, the instruments efficiently create windows around anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses single-site robotic instruments to dissect fatty lymphatic tissues from the anterior surface of the cystic artery. Hemlock clips are applied to ensure hemostasis. The visual field is widened using the robotic platform, allowing the surgeon to control the camera and instruments directly. Clips are inspected for proper positioning before a cautery hook is used to divide the tissues, ensuring hemostasis without clipping the gallbladder side.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery device for blunt dissection to identify the cystic artery, with cautery aiding in hemostasis. Calot's triangle is clearly delineated, and windows are created behind the cystic duct and artery. Hemlock clips are applied to control both the cystic duct and cystic artery.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery using Hemlock clips, ensuring secure ligation. Two clips are placed on the cystic duct for internal retention, and one clip is used to ligate the base of the gallbladder. The cystic duct is divided using scissors due to the close placement of clips. The surgeon controls back bleeding from the cystic artery with cautery, switching to the hook device for its division.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery device to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, employing a combination of blunt and cautery dissection to define the plane between the gallbladder and liver. The semi-rigid single-site robotic instruments pass through curving metal trochars, becoming less rigid as more of the instrument is exposed, resulting in some instrument whip. Careful attention is paid to avoid cautery contact with delicate structures such as the hepatic duct, common duct, or duodenum.

The surgeon reaches the top of the gallbladder, using single-site robotic instruments to manipulate and spread the gallbladder for adequate exposure of its posterior wall. The high-definition robotic camera aids in delineating the dissection plane between the liver and gallbladder. The gallbladder is nearly completely dissected from the liver, allowing inspection of the liver bed for hemostasis, with cauterization applied to a few areas. The clips are inspected and found secure, leading to the amputation of the gallbladder from the liver. The gallbladder, instruments, trocars, and single-site port are removed from the umbilical incision, marking the completion of the procedure.
